name: Jakob's Law: Follow Established Conventions
trigger: A common interface element or flow is being designed (navigation, search, cart, login, form controls, icons) and there is temptation to invent a novel pattern.
recommendation: Match the conventions users already know from the other sites and apps they spend most of their time on: place logo/home, search, and account where users expect; use standard icons with their conventional meanings; behave like the platform's native controls; and reserve novelty for genuine differentiators, not core mechanics. Conventions are a usability asset because users transfer learned expectations.
rejection: Reject reinventing standard controls without cause (custom scrollbars/selects that break expectations), repurposing well-known icons to mean something else, hiding search or navigation in non-standard places, and novelty that increases learning cost without user benefit.
validation: Compare key patterns against platform guidelines and category leaders
Run a first-click or five-second test to confirm users find core actions where expected
Flag any custom control that replaces a well-understood native one and justify the trade-off
evidence: Jakob Nielsen: 'Users spend most of their time on other sites,' so they prefer your site to work like the ones they already know
source: Jakob's Law (Nielsen); NN/g Heuristic 4 (Consistency and Standards)
